transcript.transcript: Hi, my name is Tom Brown and I am volunteering with the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project. Today is Tuesday, February 24th, 2026. I'm in my home in Seattle, Washington and I'm online with my first cousin, Allen Brown, who's at his home in Nashville, Tennessee. Today we'll talk about Allen growing up in Emmett and his experience with our family's orchards there. This oral history is conducted with support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Allen, uh, first, let me thank you for your time today. Uh, this interview will allow you to reflect on your Emmett heritage and share your experience and memories. So before before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? Yes I do. Great. And could I have you state and spell your first and last name please? Allen Brown a l l e n b r o w n. Thank you. Well, Allen, we certainly got to grow up together in Emmett. We spent a lot of time in the orchards of Allen and Hazel Brown, uh, where both of our parents had lived as well. Um, but to start, let's do an overview of your life, apart from Emmett. Uh, we graduated from Emmett High School. Uh, we spent two years together at Boise State. And then you moved to Nashville. Talk about your many years in Nashville. Jobs. What you've been doing there. So I first came to Nashville. My, uh. It was during spring break when I was in my sophomore year at Boise State, and I had found out about, um, the program, the music business program. Uh, that was, uh, B.A., Business Administration. Bachelor of Business Administration degree that Belmont honored, um. Honored Lord. I'll start over. The first time I came to Nashville was during spring break, my sophomore year at Boise State. And I had found out about Belmont having a program that was, uh, a major music business. But you had received a Bachelor of Business Administration degree, and I wanted to see what it was about and also get a lay of the land, uh, with the Nashville area. This was my first trip to the south, and I, um, once I was picked up by the admissions counselor, uh, and treated very, very much like royalty in a way, it was almost like they were, uh, he was my chauffeur, taking me to all the sights of Nashville and nice restaurants and things. And, um, the only bad thing, once I ended up in the dorm room where I was staying, it, all it had was a mattress and sheets and a pillowcase, and I had to make my bed. But the wall, I saw this huge thing crawling on it. I saw a cockroach for the very first time in my life, and it still is the largest cockroach I have ever seen. Um, so that was a little unusual. But, uh, by the time I left Nashville to come back, I knew without a doubt that was the right program for me, that the fact that at Belmont had an internship program, uh, was one of the main reasons that I decided it was a yes. But also the fact that a lot of faculty for evening classes that they had, um, were actually taught by people, some extremely well-known people, actually, in the music industry, which was excellent. Um, so I ended up coming back my freshman [Sophomore] year, um, driving with Dale Figart, who was going to a school in Missouri, and we jumped in his Honda and drove with a U-Haul. He dropped me off in Nashville. And as we're crossing the Mississippi, uh, there was this jam of traffic on a Sunday. We found out later that was because all of these people were coming for the one year anniversary of Elvis Presley's death. And, of course, they were wanting to go to Graceland in Memphis. But, uh, from that moment that started my years at Belmont, um, and I ended up interning my senior year at CBS records that later became Sony Music Nashville, was purchased by Sony, and I basically worked most of my entire career in the music industry with that label group, which included Columbia, Epic Records, and eventually also RCA, Arista, and BNA records for a moment as well, so I ended up leaving Sony in 2022. And lately, I have been doing consulting, so I'm still active in the music industry, but not as active as I was prior. So, Allen, you have officially retired, you're consulting, and what are some of the titles that you've held over the years? Um, I ended up just work wise when I came, um, to, to Belmont, my, uh, the beginning of my junior year, I was a tour guide at the Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum. Both. And I, um, met Kathy Mattea during that time, who later became a country artist, an award winning country artist, actually. So that is pretty great to have that a part of my story. But I did not become involved with the music business until my senior year when I interned at CBS Records. That title was intern in the publicity department, but then I ended up being hired right after graduation. Um, and that title I had was Coordinator of Press and Public Information, but it was also a part of the Publicity-slash-Public Relations Department. And then from there, I ended up, um, going, uh, for about 3 to 4 years. I was, uh, a part of the Contemporary Christian Gospel music, um, area. And then ended up coming back to strictly the country area. And at that point. it was still called CBS Records. But then at the same moment that I decided--for what became a 6 to 7 year stint--I decided to go into artist management, which I ended up, um. The first artist I managed was named Stacy Dean Campbell, who was a solo male country artist on Columbia, and then I ended up later managing a group called The Tractors, whose first album was double platinum for sales in excess of 2 million copies. Those were physical copies back then. Um, and, um, then I ended up coming back through Arista, and Arista became merged with RCA Label Group, which merged with Sony, which had purchased CBS Records--and voila--then for 25 more years, up until 2022, when I left, um, I was the Senior Vice President of Media and Corporate Communications. Thank you. Mhm. So I know we're going to see some threads between your, uh, successful career in Nashville and your early life in Emmett, Idaho. Um, to start your Emmett life, could you just tell us, uh, about who are your brothers and sisters And, um, what kind of work did your parents do? So I was born in 1958, July 12th, roughly two weeks after you were born, Tom. Um. And my mom and dad, Floyd and Evelyn Brown, um, they also had three other kids. And my older brother, David is 13 years older than me. My older sister Sue is. Did I say older brother because I only have one brother? My brother is 13 years older than I am. My his. I should back up. Let me just start from the beginning. I was born July 12th, 1958, two weeks after you were born. Tom and my mom and dad are were Floyd Brown and Evelyn Brown. And I grew up with three siblings. My brother, who is 13 years older than I am, is David, my older sister who is nine years older, and my younger sister, Sherry, is two years younger than I am. And Sherry is the cherry and I are the only ones that don't live in Idaho right now. Sherry lives out of the, um, Canyon Lake, Texas area that's between Austin and San Antonio, and I live in Nashville, and David lives in central Idaho. And Sue and her husband, Mel, live in Emmett. Um, but they're the only of the siblings that live there right now. Great. Um, we'll probably be talking more about your parents for sure, but, um, you and I spent a lot of time playing at Grandma and Grandpa Brown's orchards, and, uh, here, here we are [Tom holds up a photograph of Tom and Allen in their grandparents' arms]. Um, I'm being held by Hazel Wills Brown. You're Allen, you, Allen Brown, are being held by our grandfather, Allen Brown. How does it feel to have the same name as your grandfather? What was that like? No. It's incredible. I feel very privileged. Um, and I don't know why the second male got that honor. Um, David's middle name is Floyd. Like my dad, but, um. but I thought it was very great, actually. Great. And, um, I, uh, recently learned from, uh, your big sister Sue that, um, when she was very young with her big brother David, that your your family before you were born, uh, lived on the Allen and Hazel Brown Ranch in a little, little house down in the orchard. And, um, I know you have a few memories of that house. If you're like me, you and I, um, crashed the walls. Um, it was in decline, and we pushed it over the edge, I'm sure. But, um, what memories do you have of that little house and your family? Talk about living there. Well, as you say that I can kind of envision where. And I forget what you even call it. But whatever was on the outside was very worn, and I'm sure it was affixed to wood, but that it was supposed to look like brick. Maybe, but it was brown, and I don't even know if that's your recollection or not. But it. It is. But I think that it looked more like a shed than a house, and that's, uh. And it was very small. Yeah. Yeah. Um, I remember a, um, old pedal sewing machine that was in the house. Uh, but, boy, we we might have even painted walls and knocked a few out, but, uh, sorry, we desecrated it. Um. It was called. We called it the Turnbull House, and, um. Mr. Turnbull, um, was key in saving our grandfather Allen Brown's life. Um, this summer of 1962. Um, he was stung by bees. Um. And Mr. Turnbull put him in the last chance ditch, um, in the water, getting the bees away from him and, uh, saved his life. Um, um, but that was a major transition. And what are some of your first memories of Grandma and Grandpa Brown? Um. They. I know our older siblings have different views, but, uh. What what are yours? Well, now that you bring that up, because I cannot recall, um, Grandpa Brown. Prior to around that time period, because one of my earliest memories of us that connects to the accident he had was when I was four years old. And I remember it mainly because we went to Disneyland for the first time. But after we went to California from Emmett, we went to Arizona. And that's where grandpa, after that surgery, had stayed with our uncle Dick Brown and his wife, Laverne. Correct? Yep. Yes. And I remember, but I didn't put two and two together. I think yet that grandpa all of a sudden had a cane. And I remember that vividly because my cousin Rick Brown, who lived there where Grandma and Grandpa were staying, that he snuck in while Grandpa and Grandma were sleeping and borrowed the cane, and we were clowning around, like doing a vaudeville act or something. Um, just out in the living room. Everyone else had gone to bed, and I remember because I thought, oh, that's fun. My cousin, my cousin Rick is hilarious, you know? Um, and then he snuck it back in. So I, I don't know that they ever knew we had stolen his cane for a while, but then when we got back to Emmett and Grandma and Grandpa were back there, then I could tell something was different, and Grandpa had already lost his hearing. And I don't know exactly when that happened, but I, um, I'm pretty sure that happened prior to our birth. Maybe I'm not. I'm not sure when that happened, but, Grandma, I honestly do not remember when she did not have Alzheimer's. Um. And because I remember grandpa many times saying, "damn it, Hazel," to the point, I was thinking, what's her first name? But anyway, so in those years of transition, um, um, the amputation of his leg was in 1962. Um, they traveled to Arizona to visit, uh, Uncle Dick and Aunt Bernice in Phoenix. And, um, in those years, um, Doris and Dale, um, our aunt and uncle, Allen and Hazel's daughter, Doris, moved into the house. What are some of your memories of Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale and and their kids, our cousins. Yeah. Well, um, Raymond, which was a year older than you and I, Tom, that it seemed like it was very common that all three of us would hang out, especially if we were at family gatherings. Um, and many of those gatherings were at Grandma and Grandpa Brown's, where it was very much involving the orchard setting. And then there was a smaller orchard setting where, um, doors and Dale had lived before. They ended up coming over to the, to the Brown house. And, um, Dr. Doris, um, especially fond of the memories when she told me that, uh, because we were born on the same day that, um, I was the best gift she ever got, but the only gift she wasn't able to keep, which I thought that was pretty cool. Um, but. And, um, then, of course, Raymond had three other sisters, one of which is. Well, both. There are two, actually, that are on the property there now that, uh, the Brown, the Brown homestead. Yeah. So, um, talk a bit about, um, your mom and dad's work. Um, we spent time in the orchard, and, uh, I was very much aware that your dad had a business. It was fun to visit you there. We played there, too, um, on Main Street in Emmett, Idaho. What? What did your folks do? So, Dad, out of the Navy, took the trade skill he learned there with short wave radio. Um, and his love for radio and tinkering with gadgets and repairing, he started Brown's Radio Shack before the corporation Tandy Corporation even had opened its first store, called Radio Shack. That was a chain of stores eventually. But, um, then when televisions came into being, color TV, etc., dad renamed to Browns Radio and TV Shack, and, um, that retail and repair place that, um, Dad started, Mom was there as well. It was literally a mom and pop business. And then my dad, whose lifelong dream was having a radio station, was able to realize that dream. Um, around the time I was going into the ninth grade, um, um, but leaving the eighth grade and the, uh, that radio station added a whole new dynamic. And there was a they were both going on at the same time, but the my dad built a studio within the building where Brown's Radio Shack was and just condensed the area where it was the radio, TV repair area and the display area for radios and televisions and records. My dad sold LPs and 45, uh, at the retail part. And then the radio station was three different rooms, um, where the control room that overlooked Main Street, you could see through the window there and people that would walk by could look in. It was a very intriguing thing that just happened to Emmett having a first radio station in town. And then, um, there was the control area where, um, there was a significant amount of equipment in the center room, and then there was office space, um, as well. So it was very much a mom and pop business that my dad, Floyd, and my mom, Evelyn, were a part of. A quick question, um, your sister Sue, was involved in making a donation of a lot of equipment and business papers about radio station KMFE to a broadcasters museum in Idaho. I forget the name of that museum this second. I think, Idaho Broadcasters Museum. All right. Probably so. Um, so KMFE, uh, lives on and is remembered. Um, I remember, uh, studying an FCC test in order to become a DJ, and you and Raymond and I were helped by your dad, who coached us through that, uh, test I passed. Um, I didn't stay long at the radio station, but it became sort of a launching career for you. Uh, what were some of your responsibilities at KMFE? Well, yes. My soon to be brother-in-law actually worked there as well. Mel Gunter, who married Sue us and Ray, our cousin Ray worked there, and then dad and my mom also worked there. Uh, and all of us at some point were announcers. Um, even my mom on occasion. Um, and whether we were reading a very brief newscast or whatever, whatever we were doing, sometimes making commercials, um, but my dad was the main voice of the station, and dad started a weekly called it Comfy Barn Dance. KMFE were the call letters. Comfy was the slogan, Comfy Country. Um. The tagline and dad started a Comfy barn dance, which was kind of modeled after the Grand Ole Opry, uh, which also was a radio broadcast, but a live show. And there weren't a lot of audience people that would come. Occasionally, the local musicians that played there might have someone that would hang around that would help them bring in their equipment, their instruments and all that. But it was, uh, it was very special, um, you know, to have that live program every Monday night, and then it would be, um, reruns of it would be aired back on Saturdays, too. And I remember dad at our home on Saturday night when he would usually not work at all on Saturday. Um, and, uh, he would listen to Lawrence Welk. Or rather, he would watch Lawrence Welk on TV. Turn the volume down and turn up Comfy Barn Dance while I played. So and what were some of your jobs did, did did you have the same jobs all the way through or what? What role what hats did you wear at KMFE? Well, I think there was a point, whether it was official or not, that I named myself program manager, uh, which is PD. Uh, PM sorry, um, is an actual, uh, title at a lot of radio stations that I found out when I went to a record company to work for most of my career, the promotion department. Hold on one second. Sorry. I think this may be their calling. Hold on. Hello?

lines.line: # Introduction and Permissions

> Tom Brown introduces the oral history interview, providing context about the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, the date, and the participants' locations. He explains the purpose of the interview, which is to reflect on Emmett heritage and family experiences, and secures Allen Brown's permission to record the conversation. The section also includes a request for Allen to spell his name for the record.

metadata.title: Introduction and Permissions
metadata.synopsis: Tom Brown introduces the oral history interview, providing context about the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, the date, and the participants' locations. He explains the purpose of the interview, which is to reflect on Emmett heritage and family experiences, and secures Allen Brown's permission to record the conversation. The section also includes a request for Allen to spell his name for the record.

lines.line: # Allen's Move to Nashville and Career Overview

> Allen recounts his initial visit to Nashville during spring break from Boise State, his discovery of Belmont University's music business program, and his decision to transfer there. He describes his positive first impressions of Nashville, including being treated well by the admissions counselor and his memorable encounter with a large cockroach. Allen explains the appeal of Belmont's internship program and faculty connections to the music industry, which influenced his decision to move. He details his journey to Nashville, the cultural experience of crossing the Mississippi during the anniversary of Elvis Presley's death, and his subsequent career at CBS Records (later Sony Music Nashville), where he worked for most of his professional life before transitioning to consulting after leaving Sony in 2022.

metadata.title: Allen's Move to Nashville and Career Overview

He describes his positive first impressions of Nashville, including being treated well by the admissions counselor and his memorable encounter with a large cockroach. Allen explains the appeal of Belmont's internship program and faculty connections to the music industry, which influenced his decision to move. He details his journey to Nashville, the cultural experience of crossing the Mississippi during the anniversary of Elvis Presley's death, and his subsequent career at CBS Records (later Sony Music Nashville), where he worked for most of his professional life before transitioning to consulting after leaving Sony in 2022.

lines.line: # Job Titles and Career Progression

> Allen discusses the various roles he held throughout his career, starting as a tour guide at the Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum, where he met future country artist Kathy Mattea. He describes his entry into the music business as an intern in the publicity department at CBS Records, followed by a full-time position as coordinator of Press and Public Information. Allen also worked in the contemporary Christian and gospel music sector before returning to country music. He shares his experience in artist management, working with Stacy Dean Campbell and The Tractors, and his eventual return to the record label side, where he rose to senior vice president of media and corporate communications at Sony Music Nashville.

He describes his entry into the music business as an intern in the publicity department at CBS Records, followed by a full-time position as coordinator of Press and Public Information. Allen also worked in the contemporary Christian and gospel music sector before returning to country music. He shares his experience in artist management, working with Stacy Dean Campbell and The Tractors, and his eventual return to the record label side, where he rose to senior vice president of media and corporate communications at Sony Music Nashville.

lines.line: # Family Background and Siblings

> Allen provides an overview of his immediate family, including his parents Floyd and Evelyn Brown, and his three siblings: David, Sue, and Sherry. He notes the age differences between the siblings and their current locations, mentioning that only Sue remains in Emmett, while Sherry lives in Texas and David in central Idaho. Allen also clarifies the family relationships and the movement of siblings over time.

metadata.title: Family Background and Siblings
metadata.synopsis: Allen provides an overview of his immediate family, including his parents Floyd and Evelyn Brown, and his three siblings: David, Sue, and Sherry. He notes the age differences between the siblings and their current locations, mentioning that only Sue remains in Emmett, while Sherry lives in Texas and David in central Idaho. Allen also clarifies the family relationships and the movement of siblings over time.

lines.line: # Memories of the Orchard and the Turnbull House

> The conversation turns to childhood memories of playing at the family orchards and the small house on the property, known as the Turnbull House. Allen and Tom recall the house's worn exterior, its small size, and their own mischievous activities, such as painting and knocking out walls. They discuss the house's history, including its connection to Mr. Turnbull, who once saved their grandfather's life after a bee attack by immersing him in a ditch. These recollections highlight the significance of the orchard and the house in their family history.

metadata.title: Memories of the Orchard and the Turnbull House

These recollections highlight the significance of the orchard and the house in their family history.

lines.line: # Memories of Grandparents and Family Transitions

> Allen reflects on his early memories of his grandparents, particularly after his grandfather's accident and subsequent amputation. He recalls a family trip to Disneyland and Arizona, where his grandfather stayed with relatives during recovery. Allen shares anecdotes about his grandfather's use of a cane and his cousin's playful antics. He also notes his grandmother's struggle with Alzheimer's and the changes in family dynamics following the accident, including the move of Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale into the family home.

metadata.title: Memories of Grandparents and Family Transitions

He also notes his grandmother's struggle with Alzheimer's and the changes in family dynamics following the accident, including the move of Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale into the family home.

lines.line: # Aunt Doris, Uncle Dale, and Cousins

> Allen reminisces about spending time with his cousin Raymond and the close-knit nature of family gatherings at the Brown homestead. He mentions the living arrangements of Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale, and fondly recalls Doris's comment about sharing a birthday with him. Allen also notes the presence of other cousins on the property, emphasizing the strong family connections and the role of extended family in their upbringing.

metadata.title: Aunt Doris, Uncle Dale, and Cousins
metadata.synopsis: Allen reminisces about spending time with his cousin Raymond and the close-knit nature of family gatherings at the Brown homestead. He mentions the living arrangements of Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale, and fondly recalls Doris's comment about sharing a birthday with him. Allen also notes the presence of other cousins on the property, emphasizing the strong family connections and the role of extended family in their upbringing.

lines.line: # Parents' Work and Brown's Radio & TV Shack

> Allen describes his father's transition from the Navy to civilian life, using his skills in radio to start Brown's Radio Shack, which later became Brown's Radio and TV Shack as technology evolved. The business was a true mom-and-pop operation, with both parents involved in retail and repair. Allen's father fulfilled his dream of starting a radio station, KMPH, which operated out of the same building and became a significant part of the family's life and the Emmett community. The station featured a studio overlooking Main Street and sold records in addition to broadcasting.

The station featured a studio overlooking Main Street and sold records in addition to broadcasting.

lines.line: # KMPH Radio Station and Family Involvement

> The discussion highlights the legacy of KMPH radio station, including the donation of equipment and records to the Idaho Broadcasters Museum. Allen recalls how he, his cousin Raymond, and Tom were coached by his father to pass the FCC test to become DJs. He describes the various roles family members played at the station, from announcers to commercial makers, and the unique experience of working together as a family in broadcasting. The station's live program, the Comfy Barn Dance, is mentioned as a local cultural highlight.

lines.line: # Allen's Roles at KMPH and the Comfy Barn Dance

> Allen elaborates on the involvement of extended family at KMPH, including his soon-to-be brother-in-law and cousin Ray. He describes the various responsibilities he and others took on, such as announcing, reading newscasts, and making commercials. Allen discusses his father's role as the main voice of the station and the creation of the Comfy Barn Dance, a live radio show modeled after the Grand Ole Opry. He also mentions his self-appointed title of program manager, reflecting his growing interest in radio programming and management.

# Allen's Roles at KMPH and the Comfy Barn Dance

> Allen elaborates on the involvement of extended family at KMPH, including his soon-to-be brother-in-law and cousin Ray. He describes the various responsibilities he and others took on, such as announcing, reading newscasts, and making commercials. Allen discusses his father's role as the main voice of the station and the creation of the Comfy Barn Dance, a live radio show modeled after the Grand Ole Opry. He also mentions his self-appointed title of program manager, reflecting his growing interest in radio programming and management.

story.title: TBrown interview with Allen Brown 2026-02-24
story.description: In a detailed conversation, Tom Brown interviews his cousin Allen Brown about their family history and personal experiences. Highlighting involvement with the Emmett Orchards Oral History Project, Allen reflects on growing up in Emmett, Idaho, and his family's orchard heritage. As the discussion moves to Allen's career, he recounts his discovery of the music industry during a Nashville visit and his subsequent music career at Belmont University. Allen's progression in the music industry is detailed, with significant stints at CBS Records (later Sony Music Nashville) and roles such as Senior Vice President. The conversation touches upon their family's history, including Allen's parents' business ventures with Brown's Radio and TV Shack and the establishment of a local radio station. The interview weaves personal anecdotes, family dynamics, and Allen's professional journey offering rich insights into cultural and familial legacies.
